review_basis: manual page-image review: q/page-4, ms/page-3, sol/page-4

mark_scheme_working: int x^3 e^(x^2) dx = int (1/2)u e^u du
notes: method is specified; ideal test should require substitution/parts working, not only final primitive

review_basis: manual page-image review: q/page-5, ms/page-4, sol/page-5..6

notes: model wording is contextual; DE solve and logarithmic parameter solve are testable

review_basis: manual page-image review: q/page-6, ms/page-5, sol/page-7

topic: separable differential equation with trig integral

question_text: dy/dx cos^2(4x)=y, y>0. Boundary y=e^3 at x=pi/16. Show solution y=e^(1/4(11+tan 4x)).
mark_scheme_working: at x=pi/16, y=e^3 gives 3=1/4+C so C=11/4
notes: pure separable DE pathway

review_basis: manual page-image review: q/page-3, ms/page-2, sol/page-2

question: 5(a-e)
review_basis: manual page-image review: q/page-3, ms/page-3, sol/page-2
question_text: A=(7,4,0), B=(0,-3,7). L1 through A,B. L2: r=(3,-2,-4)+mu(1,2,3). Show intersection C, perpendicularity, D=(4,0,-1) lies on L2, find reflection E of D about L1.
mark_scheme_working: AB=B-A=(-7,-7,7), so L1 can use direction (1,1,-1): r=(7,4,0)+lambda(1,1,-1)
equate two components and solve: mu=2, lambda=-2
D lies on L2 with mu=1
expected_equivalent: D lies on L2 at mu=1
notes: partial: line algebra and checks are deterministic; reflection geometry step is contextual

question: 6(a-b)

notes: mark scheme accepts final derivative; tangent is deterministic

question: 7(a-c)
topic: parametric area and volume of revolution
review_basis: manual page-image review: q/page-5, ms/page-5, sol/page-4..5
question_text: Parametric curve x=cos(2theta), y=sec(theta), 0<theta<pi/2. Region under curve from x=0 to x=1/2. Show area integral int_{pi/6}^{pi/4} 4sin(theta)dtheta, evaluate exactly, then find volume when rotated about the x-axis.
mark_scheme_working: x=0 gives theta=pi/4 and x=1/2 gives theta=pi/6
area=int y dx = int_{pi/6}^{pi/4} sec(theta)*2sin(2theta)dtheta = int_{pi/6}^{pi/4} 4sin(theta)dtheta
volume=pi*int y^2 dx = pi*int sec(theta)^2*(-2sin(2theta))dtheta with limits reversed
notes: diagram only identifies bounds; integration is deterministic

notes: requires unit conversion minutes to seconds; otherwise symbolic related-rates chain rule

question: 8(a-c)
review_basis: manual page-image review: q/page-5, ms/page-4, sol/page-5
question_text: Line l: r=(a,b,10)+lambda(0,1,2). It passes through P(7,3,6). Find a,b. Point Q on l such that OQ is perpendicular to l; find Q. T is on l where lambda=-7; find ratio PQ:QT.
mark_scheme_working: 2lambda+10=6 so lambda=-2, hence a=7 and b=5
P has lambda=-2, Q has lambda=-5, T has lambda=-7, so PQ:QT=3:2
notes: partial because geometric perpendicular and distance-ratio wording is contextual; algebraic substeps are deterministic

int 6x sin3x dx = -2x cos3x - int(-2cos3x) dx

review_basis: manual page-image review: q/page-4, sol/page-5; c4_i_marks.pdf is Coming Soon placeholder
question_text: Use integration techniques to show int_0^(pi^2/4) sin(sqrt(x)) dx = N, where N is a positive integer.
mark_scheme_working: limits x=0 -> u=0 and x=pi^2/4 -> u=pi/2
integral becomes int_0^(pi/2) 2u sin u du
by parts: int 2u sin u du = -2u cos u + 2 sin u
notes: deterministic substitution and parts

question: 9(a-b)
review_basis: manual page-image review: q/page-5, sol/page-5..7; c4_i_marks.pdf is Coming Soon placeholder
question_text: Parametric curve x=6/t, y=6t-t^2, t!=0. Curve crosses x-axis at R. P(6,5) lies on C and normal l at P crosses x-axis at Q. Determine t at R and P, equation of l, coordinates R,Q, then use parametric integration to find shaded area to two decimals.
mark_scheme_working: R has y=0 so 0=t(6-t), hence t_R=6
at t=1, tangent gradient=-2/3, so normal gradient=3/2
area under curve from t=6 to t=1 is int_6^1 (6t-t^2)(-6/t^2) dt = -30+36ln6
notes: partial because final bounded region is diagram/context driven; parametric derivative, normal, intercepts and integral are deterministic

question: 1(a-b)
review_basis: manual page-image review: q/page-2, sol/page-1; c4_j_marks.pdf is Coming Soon placeholder
question_text: Curve 2cos x + tan y = 2sqrt(3). Show dy/dx=2sin x cos^2 y. Find normal at (pi/6,pi/3) in form ax+by=pi.
mark_scheme_working: differentiate wrt x: -2sin x + sec^2 y dy/dx=0
dy/dx=2sin x/sec^2 y=2sin x cos^2 y
notes: deterministic implicit derivative and normal line

review_basis: manual page-image review: q/page-2, sol/page-1; c4_j_marks.pdf is Coming Soon placeholder

int 4x cos4x dx = x sin4x - int sin4x dx
notes: straight integration by parts
